title: Charmed Life - Chapter 14 (Fragment 2)
text:
‚ÄúDon‚Äôt you understand?‚ÄĚ he said to Janet over his shoulder while he heaved at Cat to keep his head under the spray. ‚ÄúYou shouldn‚Äôt go telling him things like this until the Castle has had time to work on him. He wasn‚Äôt ready to understand. You‚Äôve given him the most appalling shock.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI‚Äôm truly enormously sorry,‚ÄĚ Janet said, crying heavily.

‚ÄúWe‚Äôll just have to make the best of it,‚ÄĚ said Chrestomanci. ‚ÄúI‚Äôll try and explain to him. Run along to the speaking tube at the end of the corridor and tell them to send me some brandy and a pot of strong tea.‚ÄĚ

As Janet raced away, Cat found himself soaking wet, with water hissing down on him. He tried to roll away from it. Someone held him in it. A voice said insistently in his ear, ‚ÄúCat. Cat, will you listen to me? Do you understand? Cat, you‚Äôve only got three lives left now.‚ÄĚ

Cat knew that voice. ‚ÄúYou told me I‚Äôd got five when you spoke to me through Miss Larkins,‚ÄĚ he grumbled.

‚ÄúYes, but you‚Äôve only got three now. You‚Äôll have to be more careful,‚ÄĚ said Chrestomanci.

Cat opened his eyes and looked up at him. Chrestomanci was fearfully wet. The usually smooth black hair was hanging over his forehead in wriggles, with drips on the ends. ‚ÄúOh. Was it you?‚ÄĚ he said.

‚ÄúYes. You took a long time recognizing me, didn‚Äôt you?‚ÄĚ said Chrestomanci. ‚ÄúBut then I didn‚Äôt know you straightaway when I saw you, either. I think you can come out of this water now.‚ÄĚ

Cat was too weak to get out of the bath alone. But Chrestomanci heaved him out, stripped off his wet clothes, dried him and wrapped him in another towel in no time at all. Cat‚Äôs legs kept folding. ‚ÄúUp you come,‚ÄĚ said Chrestomanci, and carried him again, to the blue velvet bed, and tucked him in it. ‚ÄúBetter now, Cat?‚ÄĚ

Cat lay back, limp but luxurious, and nodded. ‚ÄúThanks. You‚Äôve never called me Cat before.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúPerhaps I should have done. You just might have understood.‚ÄĚ Chrestomanci sat beside the bed, looking very serious. ‚ÄúYou do understand now?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThe book of matches was my nine lives,‚ÄĚ Cat said. ‚ÄúAnd I‚Äôve just burned one. I know it was stupid, but I didn‚Äôt believe it. How can I have nine lives?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúYou have three,‚ÄĚ said Chrestomanci. ‚ÄúGet that into your head. You did have nine. In some manner and by someone, they were put into that book of matches, and that book I am now going to put in my secret safe, sealed with the strong-est enchantments I know. But that will only stop people using them. It won‚Äôt stop you losing them yourself.‚ÄĚ

Janet came hurrying in, still tearful, but very thankful to be of use. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs coming,‚ÄĚ she said.

‚ÄúThank you,‚ÄĚ said Chrestomanci, and he gave her a long, thoughtful look. Janet was sure he was going to accuse her of not being Gwendolen, but what he said was, ‚ÄúYou may as well hear this too, in order to prevent more accidents.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúCan I get you a towel first?‚ÄĚ Janet said humbly. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre so wet.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI‚Äôm drying out, thank you,‚ÄĚ he said, smiling at her. ‚ÄúNow listen. People with nine lives are very important and very rare. They only happen when, for one reason or another, there are no counterparts of them living in any other world. Then the lives that would have been spread out over a whole set of worlds get concentrated in one person. And so do all the talents that those other eight people might have had.‚ÄĚ

vocabulary.definition: to charge someone with an offense or crime
vocabulary.example: Janet was sure he was going to accuse her of not being Gwendolen, but what he said was, ‚ÄúYou may as well hear this too, in order to prevent more accidents.

vocabulary.definition: causing shock or dismay; horrific
vocabulary.example: You‚Äôve given him the most appalling shock.

vocabulary.definition: an alcoholic spirit distilled from wine or fermented fruit juice
vocabulary.example: Run along to the speaking tube at the end of the corridor and tell them to send me some brandy and a pot of strong tea.

vocabulary.definition: gathered together in a dense or focused form
vocabulary.example: Then the lives that would have been spread out over a whole set of worlds get concentrated in one person.

vocabulary.definition: a long passage in a building from which doors lead into rooms
vocabulary.example: Run along to the speaking tube at the end of the corridor and tell them to send me some brandy and a pot of strong tea.

vocabulary.definition: a person or thing holding a similar position or function in a different place or situation
vocabulary.example: They only happen when, for one reason or another, there are no counterparts of them living in any other world.

vocabulary.definition: a magic spell or charm
vocabulary.example: In some manner and by someone, they were put into that book of matches, and that book I am now going to put in my secret safe, sealed with the strong-est enchantments I know.

vocabulary.definition: to a very great degree or extent

‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI‚Äôm truly enormously sorry,‚ÄĚ Janet said, crying heavily.

vocabulary.definition: bend (something flexible) over upon itself
vocabulary.example: Cat‚Äôs legs kept folding.

vocabulary.definition: in a persistent and demanding way

vocabulary.definition: to pay attention to sound or speech
vocabulary.example: ‚ÄúNow listen.

‚ÄĚ

Cat lay back, limp but luxurious, and nodded.

vocabulary.definition: a way in which something is done
vocabulary.example: In some manner and by someone, they were put into that book of matches, and that book I am now going to put in my secret safe, sealed with the strong-est enchantments I know.

vocabulary.definition: to move swiftly or quickly
vocabulary.example:
‚ÄĚ

As Janet raced away, Cat found himself soaking wet, with water hissing down on him.

vocabulary.definition: to identify someone or something seen before
vocabulary.example: You took a long time recognizing me, didn‚Äôt you?

vocabulary.definition: having a grave or thoughtful character
vocabulary.example: ‚ÄĚ Chrestomanci sat beside the bed, looking very serious.

vocabulary.definition: affected by a sudden upsetting or surprising event
vocabulary.example: You‚Äôve given him the most appalling shock.

vocabulary.definition: extremely wet
vocabulary.example:
‚ÄĚ

As Janet raced away, Cat found himself soaking wet, with water hissing down on him.

vocabulary.example: ‚ÄĚ he said to Janet over his shoulder while he heaved at Cat to keep his head under the spray.

vocabulary.definition: to remove clothes or covering from
vocabulary.example: But Chrestomanci heaved him out, stripped off his wet clothes, dried him and wrapped him in another towel in no time at all.

vocabulary.definition: to pull apart or into pieces by force
vocabulary.example:
‚ÄĚ

Janet came hurrying in, still tearful, but very thankful to be of use.

vocabulary.definition: to inform or narrate
vocabulary.example: ‚ÄúYou shouldn‚Äôt go telling him things like this until the Castle has had time to work on him.

vocabulary.definition: to push or fold clothes or fabric neatly into place
vocabulary.example: ‚ÄúUp you come,‚ÄĚ said Chrestomanci, and carried him again, to the blue velvet bed, and tucked him in it.

vocabulary.definition: twists and turns with quick movements
vocabulary.example: The usually smooth black hair was hanging over his forehead in wriggles, with drips on the ends.
